blocks.content: They stayed until the fireflies started to thin.
Millhaven went about its nighttime business behind them. A door somewhere. A dog twice and done. Television from an open window, too faint to make out.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I should go in.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah.
Neither of them moved.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: For what it's worth — I would have come out here eventually. Even if you hadn't shown up.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I know.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I just didn't want you to think I needed you to come.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I don't think that.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Good.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I think you wanted me to come.
He opened his mouth. Closed it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: That's different.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Is it?
She asked it lightly. Like it was easy. But she looked at the field when she said it, not at him.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: See you tomorrow.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: See you tomorrow.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Don't go to the Henderson property before you get me.
He laughed — the real one. The one that came out without permission.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I won't.
She started back down the fence line, the chain squeaking its familiar squeak, getting quieter with each second. He stood there and listened until he couldn't hear it anymore.
Then he turned back to the field. The fireflies kept going — green-gold, indifferent, each one lasting half a second and then gone. They had no idea how brief the season was.
He didn't go in until they started blinking slower.
